Secondary CTA: effort-reducing — 'See migration guide'
section_flow.psychology_notes: Naming the competitor is bold and builds instant trust. 'You already know' validates existing frustration. It says 'we understand you' before asking for anything.

section_flow.purpose: Visual side-by-side verdict
elements: Two-column table: your product vs. named competitor
6-8 comparison rows: setup time, admin overhead, key features, price at specific scale
Your column: green checks, specific numbers, accent color for best row
Competitor column: red X's, worse values, muted text
Price row LAST — the knockout punch after feature parity is established
section_flow.psychology_notes: The brain processes green-vs-red faster than reading text. The table feels objective even though it's biased toward you. Price last creates 'cherry on top' — after seeing you match on features, the price gap feels absurd.

section_flow.purpose: Prove the switch is real, safe, and beneficial
elements: Testimonial from someone who migrated from the named competitor
Must include: team size, migration timeframe, outcome metric
section_flow.psychology_notes: Handles 'what if migration is painful?' with a real story. Specific team size makes it credible. Short timeframe makes it feel easy.

section_flow.purpose: Eliminate switching fear
elements: Migration-specific guarantee: free migration, parallel running, full data export
'Keep your old tool running in parallel' is the key phrase — eliminates commitment fear
section_flow.psychology_notes: Sunk cost fallacy keeps people on bad products. 'Run both in parallel' breaks the fallacy by removing the either-or framing. 'Nothing lost' directly counters loss aversion.

section_flow.purpose: Price-anchored close
elements: Headline: 'Stop paying more for less'
CTA: 'Start free migration'
section_flow.psychology_notes: The price comparison is the final rational argument. 'Migration' not 'signup' frames the action as escaping, not adopting.

psychological_triggers._instructions: Individual conversion psychology patterns usable on ANY page type. The model should incorporate 3-5 of these per landing page, chosen based on product and audience. These are universal — they work across all industries, products, and audiences.

anchoring.what: Present a high number before presenting your price or metric. The first number becomes the reference point.
how_to_apply: Show competitor price BEFORE your price
Show cost-of-problem BEFORE product price
Show enterprise plan first in pricing pages, then Pro, then Starter
Show large usage numbers (4.2M tasks) to make the product feel massive
Show the 'before' metric (23 min wasted/hr) before the 'after' metric (4 min saved)
anchoring.placement: Metrics rows, comparison tables, pricing pages, hero subheadlines

loss_aversion.what: People feel losses 2x more than equivalent gains. Frame value as what they're LOSING without you.
how_to_apply: 'You're losing $62,400/year' hits harder than 'Save $62,400/year'
'Your competitors ship 47% faster than you' > 'Ship 47% faster'
'Every day without [product], you lose [metric]' > 'With [product], you gain [metric]'
'Stop burning 12 hours/week' > 'Save 12 hours/week'
Show what happens if they do NOTHING — make inaction feel costly
loss_aversion.placement: Hero headlines, PAS agitation sections, urgency banners, CTA micro-copy

social_proof_specificity.what: Specific social proof converts 3-4x better than generic. Names, titles, numbers, and outcomes.
how_to_apply: GOOD: 'Sarah Kim, VP Engineering at ScaleAI (420 engineers)' — name, title, company, scale
BAD: 'A VP at a large tech company'
GOOD: '2,417 teams in 43 countries'
BAD: 'Teams worldwide'
GOOD: '142 teams signed up this week'
BAD: 'Join our growing community'
GOOD: 'Velocity increased 34% in the first month'
BAD: 'Significant improvement'
social_proof_specificity.placement: Testimonials, metrics bars, hero badges, CTA headlines

risk_reversal.what: Remove the perceived risk of the decision. Stronger and more specific guarantees convert higher.
strength_hierarchy: STRONGEST: Outcome-based guarantee — 'If you don't [specific result] in [timeframe], [specific remedy]'
STRONG: Money-back with specifics — '30-day refund, no questions, no forms, processed in 24 hours'
MEDIUM: Free trial with full access — '14 days free, all features, no credit card'
risk_reversal.placement: Dedicated card near final CTA. Bordered, slightly different bg, bold headline.

cognitive_ease.what: Easier to process = more trusted. Reduce cognitive load everywhere.
how_to_apply: 3 features, not 7. 3 plans, not 5. 3 steps, not 8.
One CTA per viewport, not three competing buttons
Concrete numbers, not abstract concepts: '4 minutes' not 'quick'
Simple words: 'See your team speed' not 'Visualize velocity metrics'
Short sentences. 15 words max. Break at the period.
Visual hierarchy: the eye should follow ONE path down the page
cognitive_ease.placement: Everywhere. This is a meta-rule, not a section.

commitment_escalation.what: Small action → larger action. People who take step 1 are 5x more likely to take step 2.
how_to_apply: CTA ladder: 'See demo' (free) → 'Start trial' (email) → 'Upgrade' (payment)
Progressive forms: ask for email first → then name → then company — not all at once
Free tool or calculator on landing page → captures interest → leads to signup
Freemium: let them use the product → upsell at usage limits
Interactive elements: letting the visitor click/scroll/interact creates micro-commitments
commitment_escalation.placement: CTA strategy, signup flows, onboarding sequences

scarcity_urgency.what: Limited availability or time pressure increases perceived value. MUST be real — fake urgency destroys trust permanently.
types: TIME: 'Offer ends [specific date]' — concrete deadline, not 'limited time'
QUANTITY: 'Only [X] spots in the beta' — specific number
PRICE: 'Launching at $X — price increases to $Y after [date]'
SOCIAL: '[X] people signed up today' — implies momentum, creates FOMO
CAPACITY: 'We onboard [X] new teams per month to ensure quality'
scarcity_urgency.placement: Top banner, hero micro-copy, CTA micro-copy, pricing card badges

reciprocity.what: Give value BEFORE asking for anything. Creates psychological debt that converts.
how_to_apply: Free tool, calculator, or assessment on the landing page
Free report, template, or resource without gating
Generous free tier (not crippled trial)
Free migration/import service
Free consultation, audit, or strategy session
reciprocity.placement: Above-the-fold offer, secondary CTA, content marketing

future_pacing.what: Help the visitor mentally simulate life AFTER they buy. When someone can vividly imagine the positive outcome, the decision feels already made.
how_to_apply: 'Imagine opening your laptop Monday and seeing exactly what everyone shipped — without asking.'
'This time next month, your [metric] will be [X]% better. Your [painful activity] will take [Y minutes], not [Z].'
'In 4 minutes, your entire team's work will be in one place.'
Use present tense for the future state — 'Your dashboard shows real-time velocity' not 'will show'
future_pacing.placement: Bridge sections (between problem and solution), final CTA context, onboarding welcome screens

authority_signals.what: Institutional trust markers that signal credibility without being testimonials.
types: Compliance: SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA, ISO 27001, PCI DSS
Media: press mentions, awards, industry recognition
Scale: total users, API calls processed, data points analyzed
authority_signals.placement: Horizontal badge row, usually after hero or after features. Muted colors, small text. Supporting evidence, not the main argument.

contrast_principle.what: Things appear more different when placed next to each other. Use this to make your product look dramatically better than alternatives.
how_to_apply: Comparison tables: your green checks next to competitor red X's
Before/after scenarios: 'Without [product]' (painful) vs 'With [product]' (easy)
Price anchoring: show high competitor price before your lower price
Setup time: '2-4 weeks (them) vs 4 minutes (you)'
Pricing pages: the featured plan looks better when flanked by worse-value alternatives
contrast_principle.placement: Comparison sections, pricing pages, feature sections

endowment_effect.what: People value things they already have more than things they don't. Once they start using your product (even in trial), they won't want to give it up.
how_to_apply: Generous free trials with full access — let them build real workflows
Allow data import so their data lives in your product
Onboarding that creates immediate value — first report, first dashboard, first automation
Progress indicators: 'You've completed 3/5 setup steps' creates investment
Team invitations during trial — more people using it = harder to leave
endowment_effect.placement: Trial structure, onboarding flow design, activation strategy

animation_patterns._instructions: Animation rules for public-facing pages. Every animation must be purposeful — it either draws attention to a conversion element or provides feedback for an interaction. Decorative animation is banned.
universal_rules: Every animation must have a purpose: direct attention, provide feedback, or indicate state change. If you can't name the purpose, remove the animation.
Duration: 150ms for micro-interactions (hover, focus), 300ms for state changes (tab switch, accordion), 500ms for reveals (scroll-in), 200ms for exits (toast dismiss).
Easing: ease-out for entries (fast start, slow end — feels responsive). ease-in for exits (slow start, fast end — feels natural). Never use linear for UI — it feels robotic.
Respect prefers-reduced-motion. Wrap all animations in @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference). Users who disable motion get instant state changes.
No animation on first paint. The page loads static. Animations trigger on scroll, hover, or interaction — never on page load (except the hero badge pulse).
Maximum 2 animated elements visible at any time. If the page feels 'busy' or 'bouncy', remove animations until it doesn't.
Never animate layout properties (width, height, top, left, margin, padding). Animate only transform and opacity — everything else triggers layout recalculation and jank.

scroll_reveal.description: Elements fade/slide in as they enter the viewport. Used for feature cards, testimonials, metric numbers, and sections below the fold.
scroll_reveal.implementation: Use IntersectionObserver. Elements start with opacity: 0 and transform: translateY(20px). When they enter viewport at 20% threshold, transition to opacity: 1 and translateY(0) over 500ms ease-out.
scroll_reveal.stagger: When multiple elements reveal together (feature grid, metric row), stagger by 100ms per item. First item at 0ms, second at 100ms, third at 200ms.
rules: Only trigger ONCE per element. Don't re-animate on scroll-up.
Never reveal more than 4 items in a single stagger sequence.
Hero section is NEVER scroll-revealed — it's visible on load.
Don't reveal elements that are already in viewport on load.

counter_animation.description: Metrics/numbers count up from 0 to their final value. Used for stat cards, metrics rows, pricing numbers.
counter_animation.implementation: Trigger when element enters viewport. Count from 0 to target over 1500ms with ease-out timing (fast at start, slow at end). Use requestAnimationFrame, not setInterval.
rules: Only animate integers. Don't animate decimals (47.3%) — it looks jittery. Round to nearest integer during animation, then show final decimal value.
Prefix/suffix ($, %, +, K, M) appear immediately, not after the count.
Format with locale separators during animation (1,234 not 1234).
Maximum 4 counters visible at once.

hover_interactions.description: Feedback on interactive elements. Every clickable element must have a hover state.

patterns.buttons: transition-colors duration-150. Primary: bg lightens. Secondary: bg fills. Ghost: text brightens.
patterns.cards: transition-colors duration-150. Border color change OR bg subtle shift. NEVER scale, shadow, or lift.
patterns.links: transition-colors duration-100. Color change to accent or primary text.
patterns.nav_items: transition-colors duration-100. Text brightens + bg appears.
patterns.table_rows: transition-colors duration-100. Subtle bg fill on entire row.
patterns.images_in_grids: transform scale(1.03) over 300ms ease-out. ONLY for image galleries, not cards.

forbidden: Never combine scale + shadow + color change on one element
Never scale anything except images in galleries
Never add shadow on hover (use bg change instead)
Never change more than 2 properties simultaneously

patterns.skeleton_pulse: animate-pulse on bg-tertiary rectangles. Match shape of expected content.
patterns.button_loading: Replace text with spinner (border-2 border-current border-t-transparent rounded-full animate-spin w-4 h-4) + action verb ('Saving...', 'Sending...').
patterns.page_transition: Outgoing page opacity fades to 0 over 150ms, incoming page fades from 0 to 1 over 200ms.
patterns.toast_exit: opacity 1 → 0 over 200ms ease-in, then remove from DOM.
patterns.modal_enter: Overlay: opacity 0 → 1 over 200ms. Modal: opacity 0 scale(0.95) → opacity 1 scale(1) over 300ms ease-out.
patterns.modal_exit: Modal: opacity → 0 over 150ms. Overlay: opacity → 0 over 200ms.

attention_directors.description: Subtle animations that draw the eye to conversion elements.

patterns.pulse_dot: A small colored dot (6-8px) with infinite pulse animation (opacity 1 → 0.4 → 1 over 2s). Used on: live status badges, notification indicators, 'new' markers.
patterns.shimmer: A subtle light sweep across a CTA button on first viewport entry ONLY. 45-degree linear gradient from transparent to white/10 to transparent, translateX(-100%) → translateX(100%) over 1s. Triggers ONCE. Used only on the primary CTA, never secondary.
patterns.arrow_bounce: A small down-arrow below the hero that bounces vertically (translateY(0) → translateY(8px) → translateY(0)) over 2s infinite. Tells the visitor to scroll. Remove after first scroll event.

rules: Maximum ONE attention director visible at any time
Never on body text or secondary elements
Only on conversion elements (CTA, badge, scroll indicator)

responsive_rules_public._instructions: These responsive rules apply to ALL public-facing pages. The model implements these for EVERY page it builds — they are not suggestions, they are requirements.

breakpoints.desktop: 1024px and up — full layout, sidebar nav, multi-column

universal_responsive_rules: Mobile-first CSS. Start with mobile styles, add complexity at larger breakpoints via min-width media queries.
Touch targets: minimum 44px height on ALL interactive elements on mobile. Buttons, links, nav items, form inputs. No exceptions.
Font sizes never go below 14px (text-sm) on any screen size. Body text stays at 14-16px across all breakpoints.
Padding: p-4 (16px) on mobile page wrappers, p-6 (24px) on tablet, p-8 (32px) on desktop. Consistent within each breakpoint.
Content max-widths are enforced at every breakpoint: max-w-7xl (1280px) for full layouts, max-w-4xl (896px) for hero text, max-w-2xl (672px) for forms, max-w-sm (384px) for auth forms.
Images: always use object-cover with aspect ratios defined. Never let images stretch or distort. Use responsive srcset when possible.
Horizontal scroll is NEVER acceptable on the main page axis. If content overflows, it must wrap, stack, or be contained in a scrollable sub-container (like kanban).
Test mental model: hold your phone vertically. Can you complete the primary conversion action (signup, purchase, contact) with one thumb? If not, the mobile layout is wrong.

navigation.desktop: Full horizontal nav with links + CTA button
navigation.tablet: Hamburger menu or condensed nav (logo + CTA only)
navigation.mobile: Hamburger icon → full-screen overlay menu or slide-in drawer. CTA button remains visible.

hero_section.desktop: text-5xl heading, 14-16px body, horizontal CTA row with gap-4
hero_section.tablet: text-4xl heading, same body, CTAs may stack if narrow
hero_section.mobile: text-3xl heading, 13-14px body, CTAs stack vertically full-width, centered

feature_grids.tablet: 2-column grid (grid-cols-2) — third item wraps to new row

metrics_rows.mobile: 2x2 grid (grid-cols-2) with smaller text (text-2xl values instead of text-3xl)

testimonial_grids.mobile: Single column — cards stack. Consider horizontal scroll carousel as alternative.

comparison_tables.tablet: Table with horizontal scroll if needed, sticky first column
comparison_tables.mobile: Compact table with smaller text (text-xs), or transform into stacked cards (one per row)

pricing_cards.desktop: 3-column grid, featured card slightly elevated or bordered
pricing_cards.mobile: Single column — featured card first, others stack below

forms.desktop: 2-column field grid for short fields (name + email), full-width for textareas
forms.tablet: Same as desktop if space allows, otherwise single column
forms.mobile: Always single column. Full-width inputs. Submit button full-width.

modals.mobile: Full-width, anchored to bottom of screen (bottom sheet pattern), rounded-t-xl only

sidebar_app_layout.tablet: Collapsible sidebar (toggle button) or icon-only sidebar (w-16)
sidebar_app_layout.mobile: No sidebar visible. Hamburger menu in top bar → overlay drawer slides in from left

sticky_cta.mobile: Sticky bottom bar with CTA button, visible when hero CTA scrolls out of view. 56px height, border-top, blur backdrop.
sticky_cta.desktop: Not needed — hero CTA is visible or repeated in sections

cta_system._instructions: Universal CTA rules and formulas. The model picks the right CTA style based on conversion goal and page position.
button_text_rules: CTA text must complete 'I want to ___'. 'Start free trial' works. 'Submit' does not.
Use action verbs: Start, Get, See, Try, Join, Switch, Create, Build, Launch, Claim, Unlock.
NEVER use: Submit, Click here, Learn more (weak), Continue (vague), Next (vague).
Include the VALUE in the button when possible: 'Start shipping faster' > 'Get started'. 'See my savings' > 'Calculate'.
Primary CTA: 5-6 words max. Secondary CTA: 3-4 words max.
Add risk removal to primary CTA when possible: 'Start free — no credit card' (the dash + qualifier).
micro_copy_below_cta: No credit card required. Cancel anytime.
Import from [competitor list] in one click.
[X]-day free trial. Full features. No limits.
Your data stays yours. Export anytime.
No sales call required. Start on your own.
cta_placement_rules: Primary CTA appears minimum 3 times on a long page: hero, mid-page (after proof), bottom.
Same button text and style everywhere — don't change the CTA text between sections.
On mobile: add a sticky bottom CTA bar that appears when the hero CTA scrolls out of view.
Never put two primary CTAs next to each other. Primary + secondary (ghost/outline) only.
CTA button is ALWAYS the highest-contrast element in its section. If it doesn't pop, darken the background or increase button size.

objection_handling._instructions: Every landing page has objections the visitor is thinking but not saying. The page must handle these IN THE FLOW — not in a FAQ section. FAQs are where objections go to die. Handle them contextually, where the visitor is most likely thinking them.

is_it_hard_to_set_up.when_visitor_thinks_it: After seeing features (they imagine the implementation effort)
is_it_hard_to_set_up.handling_method: 3-step setup section with specific times and effort levels
is_it_hard_to_set_up.template: Numbered steps (1, 2, 3) with: step title + one sentence + specific time/effort marker. Example: '1. Import your data — one-click from [sources]. 2. Invite your team — SSO, Google, or email. 3. Go live — you're productive in [X] minutes.'

is_it_secure_reliable.handling_method: Trust badges row near the action point
is_it_secure_reliable.placement: After hero (for enterprise) or near forms (for consumers)
is_it_secure_reliable.template: Horizontal row: compliance badges (SOC 2, GDPR) + reliability metrics (99.9% uptime) + encryption note. Small text, muted colors.

what_if_it_doesnt_work.placement: Immediately before or alongside the final CTA
what_if_it_doesnt_work.template: Bordered card with bold headline + specific terms. The more specific and generous, the more it converts.

is_it_worth_the_price.when_visitor_thinks_it: On pricing pages and after seeing the price
is_it_worth_the_price.placement: Below pricing cards or in hero subheadline
is_it_worth_the_price.template: '[Product] costs $X/mo. The average team saves [Y hours] per [period]. At $[Z]/hr, that's $[calculated ROI] saved. ROI in [timeframe].'

is_it_too_complex_for_us.when_visitor_thinks_it: After seeing advanced features or enterprise language
is_it_too_complex_for_us.handling_method: Simplicity proof — demo screenshot, 'works out of the box' language, or beginner testimonial
is_it_too_complex_for_us.template: Testimonial from a small team: 'We're a 5-person startup and we were up and running in 10 minutes.' OR screenshot of clean, simple UI with caption 'No training needed.'

will_my_team_actually_use_it.template: '98% team adoption rate' metric + testimonials from different roles (engineer, designer, PM, manager) all saying they use it.

can_i_switch_back_if_needed.handling_method: Data portability statement and parallel-running offer
can_i_switch_back_if_needed.template: 'Your data is always yours. Full export anytime. Run [product] alongside your current tool — switch when you're ready, not before.'

placement_rules: Handle objections WHERE the visitor thinks them, not in a FAQ section at the bottom.
Security concerns → near signup forms and payment forms.
Setup difficulty → after features, before CTA.
Price worth → on pricing page, below the cards.
Team adoption → in social proof section, using cross-role testimonials.
Migration fear → in risk-reversal section, near final CTA.
An FAQ section at the bottom is acceptable ONLY for edge-case questions that don't fit the flow (billing details, API limits, data residency). The top 3-5 objections must be handled inline.

section_patterns._instructions: Reusable section patterns that can be mixed and matched across any page type. Each pattern is a self-contained conversion element.

hero_badge.what: Small pill-shaped badge above the hero headline. Creates social proof or recency before the main message.
variants: Live counter: '[X] signed up this week' with pulsing green dot — social momentum
Recency: 'New: [feature name] just launched' with sparkle icon — product momentum
Credibility: 'Rated #1 on G2 for [category]' — third-party authority
hero_badge.classes: inline-flex items-center gap-2 px-3 py-1.5 rounded-full text-xs font-medium border border-[var(--border)] bg-[var(--bg-secondary)] text-[var(--text-secondary)]

metrics_bar.what: Horizontal row of 3-4 large numbers with small labels. Creates anchoring and credibility.
rules: 3-4 metrics max. Never 5+.
Numbers: text-3xl or text-4xl, font-bold, tabular-nums, tracking-tight.
Labels: text-xs, uppercase, tracking-wider, text-tertiary.
Animate numbers on viewport entry (count up from 0).
Include unit prefix/suffix: $, %, +, K, M.
Separate with generous gaps (gap-8 to gap-12).
metric_categories: SCALE: total users, tasks completed, API calls, data processed
OUTCOME: speed improvement %, time saved, money saved, conversion rate
RELIABILITY: uptime %, response time, error rate
ADOPTION: customer count, team count, countries, NPS score
SPEED: setup time, time-to-value, onboarding duration

trust_badges_row.what: Horizontal row of small trust indicators near conversion points.
variants: COMPLIANCE: SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA, ISO 27001, PCI DSS icons/text
PAYMENT: 'Secure checkout' + card brand logos + 'SSL encrypted'
trust_badges_row.classes: flex items-center justify-center gap-4 flex-wrap py-3 text-xs text-[var(--text-tertiary)]

three_step_process.what: Visual 1-2-3 breakdown of how the product works. Reduces perceived complexity.
rules: Always 3 steps. Never 2, 4, or 5.
Each step: numbered circle + title (5 words max) + description (1 sentence).
Include a specific effort marker in at least one step: '4 minutes', 'one click', 'zero config'.
Steps should map to: INPUT (bring your stuff) → ACTION (do the thing) → OUTCOME (get the result).
Number circles: accent bg with white text for current/completed, bg-tertiary with muted text for upcoming.

comparison_table.what: Side-by-side feature comparison with a named competitor. Creates contrast and visual verdict.
rules: Name the competitor explicitly. Generic 'Others' is weak.
6-8 rows max. Don't compare 20 features — compare the ones that matter.
Your column: accent-colored header, green checks, specific values (not just checks).
Competitor column: muted header, red X's or weaker values, muted text.
Highlight your best row (usually price or setup time) with accent background.
Price comparison LAST — knockout punch after establishing feature parity.
Always use specific team size for price: '$29/mo for 50 users' not '$0.58/user'.

testimonial_card.what: Individual social proof unit. Reusable in grids, carousels, or standalone.
required_elements: Quote text: 2-3 sentences max. Must include at least ONE specific outcome (number, timeframe, or measurable result).
Author avatar: initials circle or real photo.
Author title: job title + company name.
Optional: team/company size, migration source, industry.
quote_quality_rules: GOOD: 'We migrated 200 engineers from Jira in a weekend. Velocity jumped 34% in the first month.'
GOOD: 'The automation engine saves us 12 hours a week. We automated sprint planning entirely.'
BAD: 'This product has saved us a lot of time.'
The difference: specificity. Numbers, timeframes, named features, named competitors, measurable outcomes.

risk_reversal_card.what: Bordered card that eliminates the last objection before conversion.
required_elements: Headline: bold, 3-6 words. Examples: 'Zero risk. Seriously.' / '100% money-back guarantee' / 'Switch risk-free'
Description: 1-2 sentences with SPECIFIC terms. Duration, process, coverage.
No generic 'cancel anytime' — that's expected, not differentiating.
risk_reversal_card.classes: rounded-lg border border-[var(--border)] bg-[var(--bg-secondary)] p-5 text-center max-w-md mx-auto

urgency_banner.what: Full-width banner at page top for time-sensitive offers.
required_elements: Offer description + SPECIFIC deadline + inline CTA link
Must be closeable (X button) — forced banners create resentment
Must be REAL — fake urgency permanently destroys trust
urgency_banner.classes: bg-[var(--accent)] text-white text-center py-2.5 px-4 text-sm font-medium relative

logo_bar.what: Horizontal row of customer/partner logos for passive authority.
rules: 5-7 logos max. More looks cluttered.
40-50% opacity. Logos are subconscious signals, not featured content.
All logos same height (20-28px). Maintain aspect ratios.
Horizontally centered with gap-8 to gap-12.
If you don't have real logos, use company NAMES in a monospaced or bold sans-serif font at low opacity. Still effective.
Optional: 'Trusted by teams at' or 'Used by' above in small muted text.

sticky_mobile_cta.what: Fixed bottom bar on mobile that appears when the hero CTA scrolls out of view.
required_elements: Product name or value prop (text-sm, muted)
sticky_mobile_cta.behavior: Hidden on load. Appears (slide up) when hero CTA leaves viewport. Hides (slide down) when hero CTA re-enters viewport.
sticky_mobile_cta.classes: fixed bottom-0 left-0 right-0 h-14 bg-[var(--bg-primary)]/95 backdrop-blur-sm border-t border-[var(--border)] flex items-center justify-between px-4 z-50 transition-transform duration-300

faq_accordion.what: Collapsible question-answer pairs. For edge-case questions ONLY — top objections are handled inline.
rules: 5-8 questions max. If you need more, the page copy isn't clear enough.
Questions phrased as the visitor would ask them: 'Can I import from Jira?' not 'Data import capabilities'.
Answers: 2-3 sentences max. Direct, specific, no fluff.
Place after final CTA or on a dedicated /faq page.
Never put your top 3 objections here — handle those in the page flow.
faq_accordion.question_classes: text-sm font-medium text-[var(--text-primary)] flex items-center justify-between

page_type_detection._instructions: The BUILD SPEC generator uses these keyword patterns to detect which page frameworks and psychological triggers to inject. The model doesn't choose — the pipeline detects and injects.

content_generation_rules._instructions: Rules for generating realistic, high-quality content. The model must follow these when creating sample data, testimonials, and copy for scaffold builds.
sample_data: NEVER use 'Lorem ipsum', 'John Doe', 'ACME Corp', 'Example Inc', or any obviously placeholder content.
Generate realistic names that reflect diversity: mix of genders, ethnicities, and cultural backgrounds.
Company names should sound like real companies: 'ScaleOps', 'Finbase', 'Cloudways', 'DataStack', 'Vertically' — not 'Company ABC'.
Job titles should be specific and realistic: 'VP Engineering' not 'Manager'. 'Head of Growth' not 'Employee'.
Numbers should be realistic for the product category: a SaaS tool might have 2,400 customers. A Fortune 500 product might have 200. Don't inflate to 'millions' unless it's a consumer app.
Testimonial quotes should sound like real people talking, not marketing copy: 'Should have done it years ago' not 'This product exceeds all expectations in every dimension'.
Email addresses in forms: 'sarah@company.com' not 'test@test.com'. Phone numbers: use realistic formats with area codes.
Dates and times: use realistic relative timestamps ('2 hours ago', 'Yesterday') not absolute dates unless on a calendar.
headline_quality_rules: Every headline must contain EITHER a specific number OR a specific outcome. 'Ship 47% faster' or 'The platform 2,400 teams trust'. Never 'The best project management tool'.
Headlines are 6-12 words. If you can't say it in 12 words, you don't understand it well enough.
Avoid superlatives unless backed by data: 'fastest' requires a benchmark, 'most popular' requires a ranking, '#1' requires a source.
Use the visitor's vocabulary, not internal jargon. 'See who's blocked' not 'Visualize dependency graphs'.
Test: read the headline aloud. If it sounds like a human talking to a friend, it's good. If it sounds like a press release, rewrite it.

slop_scan_patterns_conversion._instructions: Post-build regex patterns to detect conversion anti-patterns. Used by _scan_conversion_quality() in WiringAgent.

patterns.name: generic_cta_text
patterns.message: Generic CTA text detected. Use action verbs with outcomes: 'Start free trial', 'See pricing', 'Get started free'.

patterns.name: missing_social_proof
patterns.check: No element containing testimonial, review, customer count, or logo bar found on public-facing page
patterns.message: Landing page has no social proof. Add at least one testimonial, metric, or customer logo section.

patterns.name: lorem_ipsum
patterns.regex: lorem ipsum|dolor sit amet|consectetur adipiscing|ACME Corp|John Doe|Jane Doe|test@test|example\.com
patterns.message: Placeholder content detected. Replace with realistic sample data.

patterns.name: no_risk_reversal
patterns.check: No element containing guarantee, money-back, risk-free, cancel anytime, or refund
patterns.message: Conversion page missing risk-reversal element. Add a guarantee, free trial mention, or cancellation policy.

patterns.name: weak_headline
patterns.message: Hero headline contains no numbers. Add a specific metric, customer count, or outcome percentage for stronger conversion.

patterns.name: too_many_ctas
patterns.check: More than 2 button elements within a single section/viewport
patterns.message: Too many CTAs in one section. Maximum: 1 primary + 1 secondary per viewport.

patterns.name: buzzword_usage
patterns.message: Marketing buzzword detected. Replace with specific, concrete language.

patterns.name: missing_mobile_cta
patterns.check: Landing page without sticky or repeated CTA for mobile viewport
patterns.message: No mobile-optimized CTA strategy. Add sticky bottom CTA bar or ensure CTA repeats at scroll intervals.
